topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell chimed, a sound that seemed far too dramatic for a Tuesday afternoon. I shuffled to the door, my shoulders slumping slightly as I reached for the deadbolt. A box sat on the welcome mat, taped shut with harsh, industrial-grade packing tape. It was addressed to 'Apartment 3B.' I glanced at the label, then at the address on my own mailbox, a brief flicker of recognition passing over my eyes before they settled back into their usual blankness. I nudged the package with the toe of my slipper. It was heavy, unremarkable cardboard. I didn't bother opening it immediately; I just let it sit there, a temporary obstruction on my threshold. I sighed, a quiet expulsion of air that held no real weight, and turned away, heading back toward the relative quiet of my living room.

[story 2]

I picked up the package. The paper felt rough under my fingertips, and the weight was just enough to make my grip tighten momentarily before relaxing again. It was clearly meant for someone else, someone who had vacated this space months ago. I carried it into the hallway, the fluorescent light buzzing overhead casting a sickly yellow glow on the contents. I looked at the shipping label—a previous tenant's name scrawled in faded ink. There was nothing compelling about it. It wasn't urgent, it wasn't exciting. I set it down on the kitchen counter, next to the stack of unopened mail. My mind drifted to the mundane task of making coffee, the routine pulling me back to the immediate present, leaving the package to occupy a space in the periphery of my awareness.

[story 3]

When the delivery person knocked, I opened the door with minimal effort. The package was there, sitting squarely on the welcome mat. I took it without much ceremony, my movements economical and unhurried. I inspected the packaging—standard brown box, no fanfare whatsoever. I didn't feel any surge of curiosity or obligation. It was just an object now, a piece of misplaced property. I walked into the apartment, placed the box on the nearest flat surface, and immediately turned my attention to the television, flipping through channels with practiced ease. The contents of the box remained untouched, a silent testament to a life that had already moved on.
